title: Braided Vector Spaces and Arithmetic Statistics Over Function Fields
speaker: Jordan Ellenberg
abstract: The past few years have seen rapid development in arithmetic statistics over function fields over finite fields, questions like:  what is the distribution of the ell-primary part of the class group of a random quadratic extension of F_q(t)?  (Cohen-Lenstra heuristics) or:  how many G-covers of P^1/F_q are there with bounded discriminant?  (Malle's conjecture.)  Many of these results can be thought of in a unified way as questions about cohomology of the braid group with coefficients in a braided vector space.  I'll explain how this works, talk about some new results with Mark Shusterman in this vein which give novel results on prime discriminants, and point at many questions that remain only partially understood.

proof_stages.label: Number-field arithmetic-statistics targets
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with hard open questions for cubic extensions of Q—prime discriminants and Mobius cancellation—to define the statistical phenomena whose function-field analogues will be proved.

proof_stages.label: Function-field theorems for G-extensions
proof_stages.summary: For finite groups generated by one conjugacy class and for q large and prime to |G|, the speaker states the prime-conductor asymptotic for G-extensions over F_q(t) and a Mobius-sum bound over general function fields.

proof_stages.label: Braided vector spaces as compatible braid coefficients
proof_stages.summary: A braided vector space is defined by an invertible Yang-Baxter operator on V⊗V, which yields compatible B_n-representations on V^{⊗n} and hence a natural class of coefficient systems for braid-group homology.

proof_stages.label: Configuration-space and trace-formula bridge
proof_stages.summary: Configuration space Conf_n(A^1) is identified with squarefree polynomials and has braid group fundamental group, so local-system cohomology can be computed as braid-group cohomology and related by Grothendieck-Lefschetz to sums of arithmetic functions over F_q-polynomials.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic-function dictionary and tensor products
proof_stages.summary: Specific braided-vector-space coefficient systems are matched with arithmetic functions such as 1, Mobius, divisor counts, and counts of cubic covers, and tensoring coefficient systems is explained as multiplying the corresponding arithmetic functions.

proof_stages.label: Homological vanishing from invariant rings
proof_stages.summary: The needed estimates are reduced to low-degree braid homology; the invariant ring R_V=⊕_n H_0(B_n,V^{⊗n}) is used to obtain finite-dimensionality and vanishing ranges via combinatorial group theory, yielding the stated cancellation and distribution results.

examples.name: Cubic fields over Q with prime or Mobius-weighted discriminant
examples.note: The unresolved questions about cubic number fields with prime discriminant and about cancellation of the Mobius function of the discriminant motivate the function-field analogues stated next.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses cubic fields over Q with prime or Mobius-weighted discriminant as motivating open questions and states expected counts, but does not carry out a calculation on actual cubic fields.

passes.reason: The prime-discriminant subfamily has minimal one-prime ramification support, while the Mobius weighting is just a statistic.

examples.name: Random squarefree polynomial as an irreducible/n-cycle model
examples.note: Comparing a radical conductor of degree n to a squarefree polynomial, and a prime conductor to one Frobenius orbit or n-cycle, explains the q/(q−1)·1/n constant and foreshadows the Frobenius-permutation dictionary.
examples.engagement_reason: He identifies prime conductor with one Frobenius orbit on the n geometric points and computes the random-permutation/n-cycle probability as 1/n, comparing it to irreducible squarefree polynomials.

passes.reason: The irreducible polynomial/Frobenius n-cycle case is the single-factor, single-orbit extreme among degree-n squarefree factorization types.
The irreducible squarefree polynomial/Frobenius n-cycle case is the single-factor maximal-cycle boundary among degree-n factorization types.
The irreducible polynomial or n-cycle case has one factor/orbit, the extreme factorization type.
Irreducibility corresponds to a single degree-n prime factor or an n-cycle, the maximal-cycle boundary case among squarefree degree-n polynomials.
The irreducible squarefree polynomial/n-cycle case is the one-prime one-orbit extreme of factorization, used as the random model.
The irreducible case is a single prime factor, equivalently Frobenius acting as one full n-cycle, an extreme factorization type.

Used as the random model, with irreducibility/n-cycle giving the one-factor maximal-cycle extreme.

examples.name: Trivial one-dimensional braided vector space
examples.note: The case V=k with identity braiding gives the trivial braid-group action and the arithmetic function 1, serving as the simplest sanity check for the braided-vector-space-to-local-system-to-function dictionary.
examples.engagement_reason: He constructs the one-dimensional braided vector space with tau the identity, computes that all tensor powers are k with trivial braid action, and later identifies its invariant ring/function.

passes.reason: It is the minimal one-dimensional identity-braided object with completely trivial braid action.

examples.name: Binary strings and the squarefree divisor function
examples.note: The two-letter symmetric-group action on length-n strings makes the factor-through-S_n case concrete and later corresponds to the function 2^{number of prime factors} on squarefree polynomials.
examples.engagement_reason: He works through the two-dimensional switching example by describing tensor powers as binary strings, the symmetric-group action by coordinate swaps, and the corresponding divisor-count function 2^{# prime factors}.

passes.reason: Binary strings with coordinate permutations form a standard nonexceptional coefficient system realizing the squarefree divisor function.

examples.name: Exterior-algebra braided vector space and odd-degree-factor function
examples.note: This symmetric-group representation is identified with the total exterior algebra of the permutation representation and illustrates the dictionary via a function that vanishes when an even-degree factor occurs, analogous to a sum-of-two-squares condition.
examples.engagement_reason: He explicitly defines the signed two-dimensional braiding, identifies the resulting tensor powers with the total exterior algebra representation, and gives the associated odd-degree-factor arithmetic function.

passes.reason: The exterior algebra of the permutation representation is a standard signed coefficient system encoding the odd-degree-factor statistic.

examples.name: Burau representation as a polynomial-coefficient braid-family
examples.note: In response to a question about FI-module analogues, the Burau representation illustrates another compatible family of braid-group representations, contrasting polynomial-dimensional coefficient systems with the exponential ones coming from braided vector spaces.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites the Burau representation only as a standard polynomial-coefficient compatible braid-family example, without computing or applying it.

examples.name: Sign representation for marked points on mapping class groups
examples.note: An audience question proposes the sign representation of S_n on n marked points in M_{g,n} as a possible Mobius analogue, clarifying the role of point-permutation signs in the talk's Mobius mechanism.
examples.engagement_reason: In the Q&A he identifies the sign representation on marked points as the mapping-class-group analogue and states known/expected vanishing facts, but does not work through the example.
